stem:
A biologist studied pollinator visits to four native wildflower species in a meadow. The table shows the average number of bee visits per flower per hour for each species:

| Species | Bee visits/hour |
|---|---|
| Wild bergamot | 18 |
| Purple coneflower | 11 |
| Black-eyed Susan | 7 |
| Butterfly milkweed | 3 |

The biologist claims that wild bergamot is the most attractive of the four species to bees. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the biologist's claim?

choices.text: Purple coneflower averaged 11 bee visits per hour.
Butterfly milkweed averaged only 3 bee visits per hour.
Wild bergamot averaged 18 bee visits per hour, the highest rate of the four species.
The four species varied in their ability to attract bees.

explanation: The claim is about wild bergamot being the most attractive, so directly citing its highest rate (18 visits/hour) is the evidence that supports it. The other choices describe other species or a generic variation claim, none of which establishes that wild bergamot is the top performer.

stem:
A city tracked monthly ridership on its newly launched express bus route during its first four months. The table shows average weekday riders per month:

| Month | Avg weekday riders |
|---|---|
| January | 420 |
| February | 680 |
| March | 910 |
| April | 1,240 |

A transit planner claims ridership on the express route grew steadily each month after launch. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the planner's claim?

choices.text: The express bus route launched in January.
April had the highest average weekday ridership.
February's ridership was higher than January's.
Average weekday ridership rose from 420 in January to 680 in February, 910 in March, and 1,240 in April.

explanation: A claim about steady monthly growth requires showing an increase across every month, not just one comparison or a single peak. Only choice D traces ridership through all four months, establishing the month-over-month rise the planner describes.

stem:
Researchers tested the tensile strength of three recycled plastics for use in outdoor furniture. The table shows the average tensile strength in megapascals (MPa):

| Plastic | Tensile strength (MPa) |
|---|---|
| rHDPE | 26 |
| rPET | 55 |
| rPP | 34 |

A product engineer claims rPET is the strongest of the three tested plastics. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the engineer's claim?

choices.text: rPET had a tensile strength of 55 MPa, greater than both rHDPE (26 MPa) and rPP (34 MPa).
rHDPE had a tensile strength of 26 MPa.
rPP had a tensile strength of 34 MPa, higher than rHDPE.
All three recycled plastics can be used in outdoor furniture.

explanation: A claim that rPET is strongest requires showing it exceeds both of the other plastics. Only choice A cites rPET's 55 MPa alongside the lower 26 MPa of rHDPE and 34 MPa of rPP, directly establishing its top ranking.

stem:
A school surveyed students about how often they read for pleasure. The table shows the percentage of students at each grade level who reported reading for pleasure at least four days per week:

| Grade | Percent reading 4+ days/week |
|---|---|
| 6th | 62% |
| 8th | 44% |
| 10th | 29% |
| 12th | 21% |

A librarian claims that reading for pleasure declines as students move through middle and high school. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the librarian's claim?

choices.text: The percentage of students reading for pleasure four or more days per week fell from 62% in 6th grade to 21% in 12th grade.
Only 21% of 12th graders reported reading for pleasure four or more days per week.
More than half of 6th graders read for pleasure four or more days per week.
8th graders reported reading for pleasure more often than 10th graders.

explanation: A claim about decline across grades needs the drop across the full range. Only choice A pairs the 6th-grade starting figure (62%) with the 12th-grade ending figure (21%), directly showing the overall decrease the librarian describes.

stem:
A coffee shop tracked daily sales of four pastries over a week. The table shows the average number sold per day:

| Pastry | Avg sold per day |
|---|---|
| Croissant | 48 |
| Muffin | 31 |
| Scone | 22 |
| Danish | 15 |

The owner claims croissants are clearly the shop's most popular pastry. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the owner's claim?

choices.text: Muffins sold more per day than scones.
Danishes sold the fewest per day on average.
Croissants sold an average of 48 per day, more than any other pastry.
The shop tracked four different pastries during the week.

explanation: The claim is specifically about croissants being the most popular pastry. Only choice C cites croissants' 48-per-day average and notes it exceeds every other pastry, directly supporting that ranking.

stem:
A study compared sleep duration and next-day test performance for 200 high school students. The table summarizes average test scores (out of 100) by hours of sleep the night before:

| Hours of sleep | Avg test score |
|---|---|
| 4-5 | 68 |
| 6-7 | 78 |
| 8-9 | 87 |

The researchers argue that even modest increases in sleep are associated with meaningfully higher test performance. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the researchers' argument?

choices.text: Students who slept 8-9 hours had the highest average test score.
Students who slept only 4-5 hours averaged 68 on the test.
Average test scores rose from 68 for 4-5 hours of sleep to 78 for 6-7 hours and 87 for 8-9 hours, a gain of roughly 10 points per additional sleep bracket.
More sleep was associated with better scores overall.

explanation: The argument ties modest sleep gains to meaningful score gains, so the support must quantify both the sleep step and the score step. Only choice C traces all three brackets and points out the ~10-point jump per bracket, directly matching the argument's structure.

stem:
An urban ecologist measured nighttime noise levels and nesting success for a common songbird in four districts of the same city. The table shows average decibel levels and the percentage of nests that fledged at least one chick:

| District | Night noise (dB) | Nest success (%) |
|---|---|---|
| Riverside | 38 | 72 |
| Oakwood | 46 | 64 |
| Midtown | 58 | 41 |
| Port | 67 | 23 |

The ecologist argues that louder nighttime noise is associated with substantially lower nesting success. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the ecologist's argument?

choices.text: Noisier districts tended to have lower nest success.
Riverside had the quietest nights at 38 dB.
Midtown's nest success rate was 41%, between that of Oakwood and Port.
Port, the loudest district at 67 dB, had a nest success rate of only 23%, compared to 72% in Riverside, the quietest at 38 dB — a drop of nearly 50 percentage points as noise rose.

explanation: The argument links loud noise to substantially lower success. Only choice D pairs the two extreme districts' decibel levels with their nest-success rates and quantifies the ~50-point gap, giving the comparison the argument needs.

stem:
A district piloted a free-breakfast program in some of its elementary schools and compared standardized reading scores before and after. The table shows the average reading score (scale 200-400) for pilot and non-pilot schools:

| School group | Pre-pilot score | Post-pilot score |
|---|---|---|
| Pilot (free breakfast) | 262 | 284 |
| Non-pilot | 265 | 268 |

An administrator claims the breakfast program is associated with a meaningful gain in reading scores beyond what non-pilot schools experienced. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the administrator's claim?

choices.text: Pilot schools' post-pilot average was 284.
Non-pilot schools improved from 265 to 268.
Pilot schools had a slightly lower starting score than non-pilot schools.
Pilot schools improved by 22 points (262 to 284), while non-pilot schools improved by only 3 points (265 to 268).

explanation: A claim about a gain beyond the non-pilot baseline requires comparing both groups' changes. Only choice D reports the pilot gain (22 points) alongside the non-pilot gain (3 points), directly showing that the pilot's improvement was much larger than the comparison group's.

stem:
A team of materials scientists tested four insulation materials in identical test rooms. The table shows the R-value per inch (higher means better insulation) and cost per square foot:

| Material | R-value/in | Cost ($/ft²) |
|---|---|---|
| Fiberglass batt | 3.1 | 0.55 |
| Cellulose | 3.5 | 0.70 |
| Closed-cell foam | 6.5 | 2.10 |
| Mineral wool | 4.2 | 1.05 |

The team argues that closed-cell foam offers the best insulation performance of the four, though at a significantly higher cost. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the team's argument?

choices.text: Fiberglass batt is the cheapest option at $0.55 per square foot.

Closed-cell foam has the highest R-value per inch (6.5), nearly double the 3.1-3.5 of fiberglass and cellulose, but also costs $2.10/ft², roughly three to four times the cost of those cheaper options.
Closed-cell foam costs $2.10 per square foot, the highest of the four.
Mineral wool has an R-value of 4.2 per inch and costs $1.05 per square foot.

explanation: The argument has two parts — best performance and much higher cost — so the support must quantify both. Only choice B pairs the top R-value (6.5) with the cost multiple (3-4x the cheaper options), covering both halves of the argument at once.

stem:
A park service measured trail erosion depth along four hiking trails of similar age and soil type. The table shows the average erosion depth in centimeters and the average number of hikers per day:

| Trail | Erosion (cm) | Hikers/day |
|---|---|---|
| Cedar Loop | 3 | 40 |
| Birch Ridge | 7 | 120 |
| Pine Summit | 12 | 310 |
| Aspen Falls | 18 | 560 |

The service argues that trails with higher daily hiker traffic show substantially deeper erosion. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the service's argument?

choices.text: Pine Summit averaged 310 hikers per day.
Erosion depth differed among the four trails studied.
Aspen Falls, with 560 hikers per day, showed 18 cm of erosion, compared to only 3 cm on Cedar Loop, which averaged 40 hikers per day — a sixfold increase in erosion at the busiest trail.
Cedar Loop had the lowest erosion depth of the four trails.

explanation: The argument links higher traffic to substantially deeper erosion. Only choice C pairs the busiest and least-busy trails' hiker counts with their erosion depths and quantifies the sixfold jump, supplying both dimensions the argument needs.

stem:
A nutrition researcher measured the average calcium content (in milligrams per 100 grams) of four plant-based milk alternatives sold in the same grocery chain:

| Milk | Calcium (mg/100g) |
|---|---|
| Almond | 120 |
| Soy | 125 |
| Oat | 118 |
| Rice | 122 |

The researcher claims the four plant milks provide very similar amounts of calcium per serving. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the researcher's claim?

choices.text: Soy milk had the highest calcium content of the four.
All four plant milks had calcium contents within a narrow range of 118 to 125 mg per 100 g — a difference of only 7 mg between the lowest and highest.
Almond milk had 120 mg of calcium per 100 g.
Plant milks differ from dairy milk in calcium content.

explanation: A "very similar" claim is supported by showing the full range is narrow. Only choice B reports the minimum (118) and maximum (125) together and notes the 7 mg spread, directly demonstrating how tight the range is across all four milks.

stem:
A state transit agency compared average travel times (in minutes) on a commute corridor before and after adding a bus-only lane. The table shows median peak-hour travel times:

| Mode | Before lane (min) | After lane (min) |
|---|---|---|
| Bus | 42 | 27 |
| Car | 38 | 39 |

The agency argues that the bus-only lane substantially improved bus commute times without meaningfully slowing car traffic. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the agency's argument?

choices.text: Bus travel was slower than car travel before the lane was added.
Bus travel times fell from 42 to 27 minutes (a 15-minute drop), while car travel times changed only from 38 to 39 minutes.
Bus travel times dropped from 42 to 27 minutes after the lane was added.
Car travel times were 39 minutes after the lane was added.

explanation: The argument has two parts — a big bus improvement and minimal car impact — so the support must address both. Only choice B reports the 15-minute bus drop alongside the 1-minute car change, covering both halves of the agency's claim.

stem:
Marine biologists tracked the average coral cover (as a percentage of reef surface) on four Caribbean reefs in 2005 and again in 2023. The table summarizes their measurements:

| Reef | 2005 cover | 2023 cover |
|---|---|---|
| Reef A | 48% | 22% |
| Reef B | 51% | 18% |
| Reef C | 44% | 19% |
| Reef D | 47% | 15% |

The biologists claim all four reefs experienced substantial declines in coral cover over the eighteen-year period. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the biologists' claim?

choices.text: Reef D dropped from 47% to 15% coral cover, the largest absolute decline of the four reefs.
Each of the four reefs lost more than 20 percentage points of coral cover between 2005 and 2023, dropping from starting levels of 44-51% to ending levels of 15-22%.
Coral cover on Reef A fell from 48% in 2005 to 22% in 2023.
Two of the reefs had starting coral cover above 47% in 2005.

explanation: The claim is about all four reefs declining substantially, so the support must generalize across every reef. Only choice B does that, noting that each reef lost more than 20 percentage points and giving the full 44-51% → 15-22% range.

stem:
A technology journalist compared average battery life (in hours of continuous video playback) of four mid-range laptops released in the same year:

| Laptop | Battery life (hours) |
|---|---|
| Model K | 7.2 |
| Model L | 9.8 |
| Model M | 11.5 |
| Model N | 13.4 |

The journalist argues that Model N offers markedly better battery life than its direct competitors in this price tier. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the journalist's argument?

choices.text: Model M lasted 11.5 hours of continuous video playback.
Model K had the shortest battery life of the four at 7.2 hours.
Model N lasted 13.4 hours, nearly two hours longer than the next-best Model M (11.5 hours) and almost double the 7.2 hours of Model K.
All four laptops lasted more than 7 hours of video playback.

explanation: "Markedly better" requires showing Model N's lead over its competitors, not just its absolute number. Only choice C cites Model N's 13.4 hours, the gap to the next-best Model M, and the near-double comparison with Model K, establishing how large the lead is.

stem:
A county compared recycling participation rates in neighborhoods with curbside pickup to those requiring drop-off only. The table shows the percentage of households recycling at least weekly:

| Pickup type | Urban (%) | Suburban (%) | Rural (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Curbside | 71 | 68 | 55 |
| Drop-off only | 29 | 24 | 18 |

A waste-management official argues that curbside pickup is associated with much higher weekly recycling participation across every community type studied. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the official's argument?

choices.text: Curbside households participated weekly at 71% in urban areas, more than twice the rate of drop-off households.
In rural areas, curbside pickup had a 55% weekly participation rate.
Drop-off-only participation never exceeded 29% in any community type.
Weekly participation under curbside pickup was higher than under drop-off only in every community type: 71% vs. 29% in urban, 68% vs. 24% in suburban, and 55% vs. 18% in rural areas.

explanation: "Every community type" requires all three area comparisons. Only choice D reports the curbside-vs-drop-off gap in urban, suburban, and rural neighborhoods, directly supporting the across-the-board claim.

stem:
A university compared first-year retention rates of students in three types of freshman seminars. The table shows enrollment and the percentage of students still enrolled at the start of their second year:

| Seminar type | Enrolled | Retained to year 2 (%) |
|---|---|---|
| Peer-led small group | 1,200 | 89 |
| Faculty lecture | 1,500 | 74 |
| Online asynchronous | 900 | 63 |

An education researcher argues that peer-led small groups are associated with the highest retention and that the advantage over the next-best format is large enough to matter at the scale of the university's freshman class. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the researcher's argument?

choices.text: The online asynchronous seminar had the lowest retention rate at 63%.
The peer-led small-group seminar retained 89% of its students.
The faculty lecture enrolled more students than the peer-led small group.

The peer-led small-group seminar retained 89% of its 1,200 students to year 2 — 15 percentage points more than the 74% retained by the faculty-lecture format, a gap that would translate to roughly 180 additional students retained per thousand at the larger scale.

explanation: The argument has two parts — highest retention and a gap that matters at scale — so support must cite the top rate, the next-best rate, and quantify the scale impact. Only choice D does all three, pairing the 89% vs. 74% comparison with the ~180-students-per-thousand estimate.

stem:
A public-health team studied influenza vaccination rates and hospital admissions for flu-related illness in four neighboring counties during a single flu season. The table shows vaccination coverage and hospital admissions per 10,000 residents:

| County | Vaccinated (%) | Flu admissions / 10,000 |
|---|---|---|
| Ashford | 38 | 92 |
| Belton | 47 | 68 |
| Cresthill | 62 | 41 |
| Dunmore | 74 | 22 |

The team argues that higher vaccination coverage is associated with substantially fewer flu-related hospital admissions, not just a modest decrease. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the team's argument?

choices.text: Dunmore had the highest vaccination rate and the lowest hospital admissions.
Flu admissions varied from 22 to 92 per 10,000 residents across the four counties.
Belton had a higher vaccination rate than Ashford and fewer admissions.
Ashford, with only 38% vaccination coverage, had 92 flu admissions per 10,000 residents, while Dunmore, with 74% coverage, had only 22 — roughly a quarter of Ashford's rate.

explanation: "Substantially fewer, not just modest" requires both the vaccination and admissions figures for the extreme counties plus a sense of the magnitude. Only choice D supplies all three — Ashford's 38%/92, Dunmore's 74%/22, and the roughly one-quarter comparison.

stem:
An agricultural scientist compared water use and yield for a drought-tolerant wheat variety against a conventional variety across three trial fields in the same region. The table shows irrigation water applied (in liters per kilogram of harvested grain) and total grain yield (in kilograms per hectare):

| Field | Variety | Water (L/kg) | Yield (kg/ha) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Drought-tolerant | 480 | 3,100 |
| 1 | Conventional | 780 | 3,250 |
| 2 | Drought-tolerant | 510 | 2,950 |
| 2 | Conventional | 820 | 3,080 |
| 3 | Drought-tolerant | 495 | 3,020 |
| 3 | Conventional | 795 | 3,180 |

The scientist argues that the drought-tolerant variety uses substantially less water per kilogram of grain while producing yields close to those of the conventional variety. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the scientist's argument?

choices.text: The drought-tolerant variety yielded between 2,950 and 3,100 kg/ha across the three fields.
In Field 1, the drought-tolerant variety used 480 L/kg of water and the conventional variety used 780 L/kg.
The conventional variety had a higher yield than the drought-tolerant variety in every field.

Across all three fields, the drought-tolerant variety used 480-510 liters of water per kg of grain — roughly 35-40% less than the conventional variety's 780-820 L/kg — while its yields (2,950-3,100 kg/ha) came within about 5% of the conventional yields (3,080-3,250 kg/ha).

explanation: The argument has two parts — substantially less water and close-to-equal yield — across all three fields, so support must quantify both dimensions across the full dataset. Only choice D does, pairing the 35-40% water reduction with the within-5% yield comparison over the whole trial.

stem:
A civic-engagement researcher analyzed voter turnout in local elections for towns that had adopted mail-in ballots versus towns that had retained in-person-only voting. The table shows average turnout (as a percentage of eligible voters) across two election cycles:

| Voting method | 2020 turnout | 2024 turnout |
|---|---|---|
| Mail-in ballot | 58% | 64% |
| In-person only | 41% | 43% |

The researcher argues that towns adopting mail-in ballots not only had higher baseline turnout but also saw a larger gain in turnout between 2020 and 2024 than in-person-only towns. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the researcher's argument?

choices.text: Mail-in ballot towns averaged 64% turnout in 2024, compared to 43% for in-person-only towns.
In-person-only towns saw their turnout rise from 41% in 2020 to 43% in 2024.
Turnout in mail-in ballot towns was higher than 50% in both election cycles.

Mail-in ballot towns had higher turnout than in-person-only towns in both 2020 (58% vs. 41%) and 2024 (64% vs. 43%), and their turnout rose 6 percentage points between the two elections compared to a gain of just 2 percentage points for in-person-only towns.

explanation: The argument has two claims — higher baseline and larger gain — so support must show both levels and both changes. Only choice D does, reporting the 2020 and 2024 turnout for both groups and the 6-point vs. 2-point gain comparison.

stem:
An economist compared weekly grocery spending before and after a city adopted a sugar-sweetened beverage tax, tracking purchases of taxed beverages and untaxed alternatives. The table summarizes the average household weekly spending in dollars:

| Category | Pre-tax spending | Post-tax spending |
|---|---|---|
| Taxed sugary drinks | 8.40 | 4.90 |
| Untaxed water/seltzer | 2.10 | 4.30 |
| Untaxed diet drinks | 1.80 | 2.50 |

The economist argues that the tax was associated with a sharp drop in sugary-drink spending that was substantially offset — though not fully replaced — by increased spending on untaxed alternatives. Which choice most effectively uses data from the table to support the economist's argument?

choices.text: Weekly spending on taxed sugary drinks dropped by $3.50 after the tax took effect.
Spending on untaxed water and seltzer more than doubled, rising from $2.10 to $4.30 per week.
Post-tax spending on taxed sugary drinks was $4.90, still higher than spending on either category of untaxed alternative.

Spending on taxed sugary drinks fell from $8.40 to $4.90 per week — a drop of $3.50 — while spending on untaxed water/seltzer and diet drinks rose by $2.20 and $0.70 respectively (a combined $2.90 increase), offsetting most but not all of the decline.

explanation: The argument combines the size of the drop, the size of the substitution, and the fact that substitution is incomplete. Only choice D quantifies all three — the $3.50 decrease in sugary-drink spending, the $2.90 combined rise in untaxed alternatives, and the remaining gap between them.
